name: Rocca Paolina
architectural_style: Renaissance papal punitive fortress built directly over a buried medieval quarter — Antonio da Sangallo the Younger designed the Rocca Paolina between 1540 and 1543 on commission from Pope Paul III following Perugia's defeat in the Salt War; the fortress was built not by clearing the existing Baglioni family district but by filling it: the streets, house fronts, and ground floors of the Baglioni quarter were preserved intact beneath the new fortress walls, effectively creating a buried medieval city entombed within the fortress foundations; the portion visible today is the surviving underground level — the original Baglioni streets, tower bases, and house interiors — which is traversed by modern public escalators connecting the lower town to Piazza Italia; the above-ground fortress was almost completely demolished by the citizens of Perugia in 1848 following the end of papal rule, leaving only fragments of the outer walls

opening_hours.monday: Open — the underground passage is a public pedestrian route
opening_hours.seasonal_note: The underground passage connecting the lower town to Piazza Italia, including the escalators through the buried Rocca Paolina, is a free public pedestrian route open daily and used by Perugia residents as part of their ordinary commute. No ticket is required to walk through the underground streets and use the escalators. The exhibition spaces within the Rocca, when active, may carry a separate admission fee. The GYG Perugia Old Town Walking Tour (t410012, 4.7★/341 reviews, from $34) includes a guided visit 'underground in the Rocca Paolina' as part of a 1.5-hour circuit of the historic centre — the $34 covers the guide and the full walking tour, including the Rocca underground section, the Etruscan Arch, Piazza IV Novembre, and the Fontana Maggiore.

visit_duration: 30 minutes (free public walk-through); 1.5 hours (GYG guided walking tour of Perugia historic centre including the underground section)
best_season: Year-round — the underground portion maintains a consistent cool temperature regardless of season; the free public passage makes it accessible at any time; the guided walking tour of Perugia's historic centre is best April to October
accessibility: The Rocca Paolina underground passage is equipped with public escalators (the same escalators that connect the lower town to Piazza Italia) and is accessible for visitors with limited mobility along the main escalator route. Some secondary passages involve stairs. The underground is well lit.
tagline: Pope Paul III's punishment: bury the enemy's medieval streets under a fortress — Perugia kept the streets and tore down the fortress in 1848, leaving a free public passage through the buried medieval city
highlights: The buried medieval city — the Baglioni family's actual 15th-century streets, house facades, tower bases, and ground-floor interiors survive intact beneath the fortress that was built to entomb them; walking through the underground passage you are walking through the streets of pre-conquest Perugia preserved by accident of construction method: Pope Paul III had the fortress built over the medieval quarter rather than clearing it, so the district was sealed rather than destroyed

The Salt War of 1540: why this fortress exists — Perugia revolted in 1540 against a new papal salt tax (the 'Guerra del Sale'), lost the revolt when the city capitulated after a brief siege, and was punished by the construction of the Rocca directly on top of the Baglioni quarter — the ruling family's district demolished (or rather buried) as a permanent symbol of who now controlled the city; the fortress physically embodied papal authority for three centuries

Free public escalators through a Renaissance fortress — the Rocca Paolina underground is not only a heritage site but an active civic infrastructure: modern escalators installed in the 20th century use the fortress's buried passages to connect the lower town to Piazza Italia, and Perugians use it daily as a commuter route; the conjunction of Renaissance military architecture and contemporary public escalators is unlike anything else in Italian historic cities

Antonio da Sangallo the Younger's entombment technique — rather than demolishing the Baglioni district, Sangallo's design filled the existing rooms and streets with rubble and soil to the level of the new fortress floor, then built on top; the filling preserved the below-grade structures while erasing them from the urban surface; it is arguably the most thorough act of punitive urban erasure in Italian Renaissance history — and the most complete accidental preservation

1848: the demolition as a political act — when Perugia came under Risorgimento control during the revolutions of 1848, the citizens immediately attacked the above-ground portion of the Rocca; the demolition of the fortress was not primarily practical but symbolic: tearing down the building that had represented three centuries of papal dominance over the city was an act of political theatre that left the underground portion — embedded in the hillside below street level — untouched by necessity
GYG Perugia Old Town Walking Tour including Rocca Paolina underground (t410012, 4.7★/341 reviews, from $34, 1.5 hours) — the most-reviewed GYG product on the site
description:
The most remarkable thing about the Rocca Paolina is that it was meant to erase something and instead preserved it. Pope Paul III commissioned Antonio da Sangallo the Younger to build a punitive fortress on top of the Baglioni family's quarter of Perugia after the city's defeat in the Salt War of 1540 — a revolt against a new papal salt tax that ended with the city's capitulation and the pope's decision to make the consequences physically permanent. The fortress would occupy the highest point of the city, built directly over the homes and streets of the family who had run the city for decades, eliminating their district from the urban map. It stood for three centuries as the most visible symbol of papal control over Perugia. Then Perugia tore it down.

What survives is the part that was buried.

Sangallo's construction method, dictated by the terrain and the existing buildings, was not to demolish the Baglioni district but to fill it. The streets and house fronts, the tower bases and ground-floor rooms of the quarter, were filled with rubble and soil to the level of the new fortress floor, then built over. The district was sealed rather than cleared — an accidental form of preservation in which the very act of entombment kept the medieval urban fabric intact beneath the weight of the fortress above it.

When Perugia gained independence from papal control during the upheavals of 1848, the citizens attacked the above-ground portion of the fortress — a specific act of political demolition, aimed at the symbol of papal power — and dismantled most of what rose above the hillside. The underground portion was embedded in the rock below street level and could not be demolished without destroying the hill itself. It remained, sealed, until the 20th century, when the city excavated and opened the buried passages as a pedestrian route between the lower town and the upper city, installing modern escalators through the fortress passages.

The result is something that has no direct equivalent in Italy. You enter the Rocca Paolina from the lower town through an entrance cut into the hillside, and you are immediately in the buried 15th-century Baglioni quarter: actual medieval streets with house facades still standing to first-floor height, tower bases, doorways, window openings, arched passages between buildings — the entire social geography of a pre-conquest medieval urban district preserved at scale beneath the fortress that was built to erase it from collective memory. The streets are lit, signed, and maintained by the city. The escalators that carry Perugians between the lower town and Piazza Italia run through the same passages.

The Salt War that produced the fortress deserves its context. In 1539, Pope Paul III imposed a new tax on salt throughout the Papal States. The Perugians, who had a specific exemption from salt taxes under an earlier treaty, refused to pay and expelled the papal legate. The Pope's response was military force; the city capitulated in the summer of 1540 without significant fighting. The punishment Paul III chose — not execution or exile but the physical obliteration of the ruling family's district from the urban fabric — was specifically architectural. A fortress built over your houses, streets, and towers, erasing your district from the surface of the city while leaving you aware that it existed beneath: the Rocca Paolina was a form of permanent humiliation that used buildings as its medium.

The buildings below are also a social document. The Baglioni quarter preserved under the fortress contains the street plan of a 15th-century Perugian upper-class district: the relationship between the main thoroughfares and secondary lanes, the relative scale of tower houses and ordinary residences, the placement of arched passages — the spatial sociology of medieval urban life at the level of one neighborhood. Walking through it is as close as it is possible to get to the experience of walking through a medieval Italian town, with the obvious difference that it is underground, artificially lit, and serves as a commuter route for contemporary Perugians.

The guided walking tour offered through GYG (t410012, 4.7★/341 reviews, from $34) covers the underground section as part of a broader 1.5-hour Perugia historic-centre tour that also visits the Etruscan Arch, Piazza IV Novembre, and the Fontana Maggiore — one of the finest medieval fountains in Italy. The Rocca underground walk-through is free as a public pedestrian passage at any time.

[Cardaneto Castle](/castles/italy/cardaneto-castle) in Foligno, 35 kilometres southeast in the same Umbrian valley system, is the region's other medieval fortification on this site.
history: 1540: Perugia revolts against Pope Paul III's salt tax — the 'Guerra del Sale'; the city capitulates after a short siege. 1540: Pope Paul III commissions Antonio da Sangallo the Younger to build a punitive fortress directly over the Baglioni family's quarter. 1540–1543: Rocca Paolina constructed; the Baglioni district's streets and house fronts are filled and built over rather than demolished; the Porta Marzia, a Roman gate incorporated into the fortress wall, is preserved. 1540–1848: The above-ground fortress stands as the symbol of papal control over Perugia for three centuries. 1848: Perugia gains independence during the Risorgimento uprisings; citizens demolish most of the above-ground fortress as a political act; the underground level survives intact below street level. Late 19th–20th century: The underground passages are gradually excavated and opened to the public. 20th century: Escalators installed to use the underground passages as a public pedestrian connection between the lower town and Piazza Italia. Present day: The underground passage is a free public route open daily; exhibition spaces within the walls host rotating cultural programming.
how_to_visit:
**Free public walk-through (no ticket required):** Enter from Via Baglioni (lower town) or from Piazza Italia (upper city). The underground passage is open daily and used as a regular pedestrian route. Escalators connect the two levels. Allow 20–30 minutes for a self-directed walk.

**GYG Perugia Old Town Walking Tour including Rocca Paolina underground (~$34, t410012, 4.7★/341 reviews, 1.5 hours):** Guided walking tour of Perugia's historic centre with a guided underground section in the Rocca plus the Etruscan Arch, Piazza IV Novembre, and Fontana Maggiore. The $34 covers the full 1.5-hour guided tour.

**Getting there:** Perugia is approximately 2.5 hours from Rome by train (Trenitalia from Roma Termini to Perugia). The Rocca Paolina entrance is a short walk from the main escalator system that serves the historic centre.

faqs.question: Do I need a ticket to visit the Rocca Paolina?
faqs.answer: No — the underground passage through the Rocca Paolina is a free public pedestrian route that Perugians use daily to travel between the lower town and Piazza Italia. The escalators and the medieval street passages they run through are civic infrastructure, not a ticketed museum. Exhibition spaces within the wider Rocca Paolina complex may charge a separate admission fee when in use for cultural events. The GYG guided walking tour (from $34) adds a guide and a broader Perugia historic-centre itinerary to the underground section; the underground portion itself requires no ticket at any time.

faqs.question: What exactly survived the 1848 demolition?
faqs.answer: The 1848 demolition targeted the above-ground portions of the Rocca Paolina — the fortress walls, towers, and battlements that rose above the hilltop as visible symbols of papal control. These were substantially dismantled. What survived is the underground level: the buried 15th-century Baglioni streets and house facades that the 1540 construction had filled and sealed rather than cleared. Because they were embedded in the hillside below street level, the 1848 demolition could not reach them without undermining the hill itself. One significant surface remnant also survived: the Porta Marzia, a Roman gate that Sangallo incorporated into the outer fortress wall and which still stands in partial form as the most visible above-ground remainder of the original construction.

hero_image.alt: Rocca Paolina — the buried underground medieval streets and escalators of the papal fortress built over the Baglioni quarter after Perugia's 1540 Salt War defeat, Perugia, Umbria, Italy

meta_description: Walk free through Rocca Paolina — the underground medieval streets buried by a papal fortress in 1540 and demolished in 1848, now a public escalator route through Perugia's buried past.

The underground passage is also freely accessible to the public at any time without a ticket or tour.
